quality.rationale: 完整度规降维+正交标架求曲率+Gauss-Bonnet拓扑验证+明确物理背景

origin.question: Problem 17.19. Calculate the Gaussian curvature of the horizon of a Kerr black hole and show that it becomes negative for $a > 3^{\\frac{1}{2}}\\mathbf{M} / 2$ . (This shows that the horizon cannot be globally embedded in a Euclidean 3-space if $a > 3^{\\frac{1}{2}}\\mathbf{M} / 2$ .) Use the Gauss-Bonnet theorem to check that the horizon is topologically a 2-sphere.
origin.solution:
Solution 17.19. In Boyer-Lindquist coordinates, for $t =$ constant and $r = r_+$ , the Kerr metric gives the metric of the horizon:
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \left(\mathrm {r}_{+}^{2} + \mathrm {a}^{2} \cos^{2} \theta\right) \mathrm {d} \theta^{2} + \left(2 \mathrm {M r}_{+}\right)^{2} \sin^{2} \theta \left(\mathrm {r}_{+}^{2} + \mathrm {a}^{2} \cos^{2} \theta\right)^{- 1} \mathrm {d} \phi^{2}. \tag {1}
$$
(We have used the relation $\mathbf{r}_+^2 + \mathbf{a}^2 = 2\mathbf{M}\mathbf{r}_+$ in $\mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi}$ .) The Gaussian curvature $K$ of a 2-surface is the same as the Riemannian curvature of Problem 9.23, where the Riemann tensor is computed from the
2-dimensional metric. It is convenient to use an orthonormal basis dyad. For a metric of the form
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {e}^{2 a_{1}} \left(\mathrm {d x}^{1}\right)^{2} + \mathrm {e}^{2 a_{2}} \left(\mathrm {d x}^{2}\right)^{2} \tag {2}
$$
we let
$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}^{\hat {1}} = \mathrm {e}^{\alpha_{1}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}}^{1}, \quad \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}^{\hat {2}} = \mathrm {e}^{\alpha_{2}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}}^{2} \tag {3}
$$
and we have
$$
\mathrm {K} = \mathrm {R}_{\hat {1} \hat {2} \hat {1} \hat {2}} = \mathrm {R}_{1 2 1 2} \mathrm {e}^{- 2 \left(\alpha_{1} + \alpha_{2}\right)}. \tag {4}
$$
The quickest way to find the Riemann tensor is with forms as in Problems 8.27 and 9.20. Since
$$
\mathbf {d} \widetilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}^{\widehat {1}} = \mathrm {e}^{\alpha_{1}} \widetilde {\mathbf {d}}_{\alpha_{1}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}}^{1} = \mathrm {e}^{\alpha_{1}} \alpha_{1, 2} \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}}^{2} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}}^{1} = \alpha_{1, 2} \mathrm {e}^{- \alpha_{2}} \widetilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}^{\widehat {2}} \wedge \widetilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}^{\widehat {1}}
$$
and a similar expression with 1 and 2 interchanged, we find
$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}_{\hat {2}}^{\hat {1}} = \mathrm {e}^{- a_{2}} a_{1, 2} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}^{\hat {1}} - \mathrm {e}^{- a_{1}} a_{2, 1} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}^{\hat {2}}. \tag {5}
$$
(Recall that $\mathbf{d}\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\alpha}} = -\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\alpha}}\hat{\beta} \wedge \tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\beta}}$ and $	ilde{\pmb{\omega}}_{\hat{\alpha}}\hat{\beta} = -\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}_{\hat{\beta}}\hat{\alpha}$ .) The only nontrivial curvature form is
$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {R}_{\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {2}}}}^{\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {1}}}} = \mathbf {d} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}_{\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {2}}}}^{\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {1}}}} + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}_{\widehat {\widehat {\boldsymbol {\alpha}}}}^{\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {1}}}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}_{\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {2}}}}^{\widehat {\widehat {\boldsymbol {\alpha}}}} \\= \mathrm {d} \left(a_{1, 2} \mathrm {e}^{a_{1} - a_{2}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}}^{1} - a_{2, 1} \mathrm {e}^{a_{2} - a_{1}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}}^{2}\right) + 0 \\= \left. \left(a_{1, 2} e^{a_{1} - a_{2}}\right)_{, 2} \widetilde {d x}^{2} \wedge \widetilde {d x}^{1} - \left(a_{2, 1} e^{a_{2} - a_{1}}\right)_{, 1} \widetilde {d x}^{1} \wedge \widetilde {d x}^{2} \right. \\= - \mathrm {e}^{- (\alpha_{1} + \alpha_{2})} \left[ \left(\mathrm {e}_{, 1}^{\alpha_{2}} \mathrm {e}^{- \alpha_{1}}\right)_{, 1} + \left(\mathrm {e}_{, 2}^{\alpha_{1}} \mathrm {e}^{- \alpha_{2}}\right)_{, 2} \right] \tilde{\omega}^{\hat {1}} \wedge \tilde{\omega}^{\hat {2}}. \ \\end{array}
$$
Since $\mathcal{R}_{\hat{1}\hat{2}} = \mathbf{R}_{\hat{1}\hat{2}\hat{1}\hat{2}}\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{1}}\wedge \tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{2}}$ we have
$$
\mathrm {K} = \mathrm {R}_{\hat {1} \hat {2} \hat {1} \hat {2}} = - \mathrm {e}^{- \left(\alpha_{1} + \alpha_{2}\right)} \left[ \left(\mathrm {e}_{, 1}^{\alpha_{2}} \mathrm {e}^{- \alpha_{1}}\right)_{, 1} + \left(\mathrm {e}_{, 2}^{\alpha_{1}} \mathrm {e}^{- \alpha_{2}}\right)_{, 2} \right]. \tag {6}
$$
Using the explicit form of the metric in Equation (1), with $	heta = \mathbf{x}^1$ and $\phi = \mathbf{x}^2$ , we find from Equation (6) that
$$
K = 2 M r_{+} \frac {r_{+}^{2} - 3 a^{2} \cos^{2} \theta} {\left( r_{+}^{2} + a^{2} \cos^{2} \theta \right)^{3}}. \tag {7}
$$
Note that if $r_+^2 - 3a^2 < 0$ , then $K$ is negative for a range of $	heta$ around the poles $(\theta = 0, \pi)$ . But when $K$ is negative, the surface cannot be globally embedded in a Euclidean 3-space. Since $r_+ = M + (M^2 - a^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ , the condition that $r_+^2 - 3a^2$ be less than zero is equivalent to $a > 3^{\frac{1}{2}}M / 2$ .
The Gauss-Bonnet theorem says that
$$
\int \mathrm {K d}^{2} \mathrm {S} = 2 \pi \chi
$$
where $\chi$ is the Euler characteristic of the surface ( $\chi = 2$ for a sphere, 0 for a torus, etc.). Since
$$
\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathrm {S} = (\mathrm {g} )^{\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi = 2 \mathrm {M r}_{+} \sin \theta \mathrm {d} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi
$$
we have
$$
\begin{array}{l} \chi = (2 \pi )^{- 1} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} d \phi \int_{0}^{\pi} (2 M r_{+})^{2} \frac {r_{+}^{2} - 3 a^{2} \cos^{2} \theta}{\left( r_{+}^{2} + a^{2} \cos^{2} \theta \right)^{3}} \sin \theta d \theta \\= (2 \mathrm {M r}_{+})^{2} 2 \int_{0}^{1} \frac {\mathrm {r}_{+}^{2} - 3 a^{2} x^{2}}{\left( \mathrm {r}_{+}^{2} + a^{2} x^{2} \right)^{3}} d x \\= (2 \mathrm {M r}_{+})^{2} 2 \left[ \frac {x}{\left( \mathrm {r}_{+}^{2} + \mathrm {a}^{2} x^{2} \right)^{2}} \right]_{0}^{1} \\= 2 \ \\end{array}
$$
so the surface is topologically a 2-sphere. (For further details see L. Smarr, Phys. Rev. D. 7, 289, (1973).)
